chunk_summary: Guidelines for initiating oral nutritional supplements (ONS) and transitioning from enteral tube feeding to oral feeding, with criteria for escalation and step-down targets in pediatric patients.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for initiating oral nutritional supplements (ONS) in pediatric patients.
Identify indications for enteral tube feeding and appropriate feeding modalities (e.g., nasogastric vs. gastrostomy).
Learn step-down targets for transitioning from tube feeding to oral feeding.
Recognize the importance of maintaining oral stimulation during tube feeding to preserve feeding skills.
key_facts: ONS is recommended when intake remains below needs despite a meal plan, moderate underweight or wasting with poor appetite, or increased requirements due to medical conditions like CHD or chronic lung disease.
Enteral tube feeding is indicated when oral intake is unsafe or persistently inadequate, such as <60% of needs over several days despite coaching and ONS.
Nasogastric (NG) feeding is suitable for short-to-medium duration, while gastrostomy is preferred for long-term feeding support.
Step-down targets include achieving >75% oral intake for several days, manageable meal durations, and improved weight velocity.
Reassess feeding plans every 2–4 weeks and maintain oral stimulation during tube feeding to prevent skill loss.
reference_quote: Enteral tube feeding is indicated when oral intake is unsafe or persistently inadequate. High-yield triggers include aspiration risk not controllable with pacing or texture modification, severe wasting (including SAM) with poor oral intake, prolonged feeding times with fatigue, or failure to meet a meaningful proportion of estimated needs (for example consistently <60% over several days) despite coaching and ONS.
document_key_topics: Step-down targets for transitioning to oral feeding
Preservation of feeding skills during tube feeding
